title: QUIC Protocol
description: Google's modern transport protocol over UDP — eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking and reduces connection latency.
fullExplanation: QUIC (Quick UDP Internet Connections) is a transport protocol built on UDP that provides TCP-like reliability with better performance. It was developed by Google in 2012, standardized as RFC 9000 in 2021, and is the foundation of HTTP/3. QUIC multiplexes multiple streams over a single connection — unlike TCP, a lost packet only blocks the stream it belongs to, not all streams (no head-of-line blocking). QUIC also integrates TLS 1.3 — the handshake establishes both transport and security in 1 RTT (0-RTT for repeat connections).

advantages.description: Each stream is independent — lost packet blocks only that stream, not others
QUIC + TLS 1.3 handshake combined — connection ready in 1 RTT vs TCP's 3 RTT (TCP + TLS 1.3)
Repeat connections can send data immediately — 0 RTT overhead
Connection ID survives IP/port changes — seamless WiFi to cellular handoff
Better ACK handling, packet number spaces per encryption level

versions.feature: Google's original QUIC — proprietary, used in Chrome/YouTube
HTTP over QUIC — replaces HTTP/2 over TCP

keyFacts: QUIC runs over UDP port 443 — same as HTTPS
HTTP/3 uses QUIC — ~25% of web traffic as of 2024
QUIC header is encrypted — middleboxes can't inspect or modify it
Connection ID: 64-bit identifier survives IP address changes
QUIC streams: independent byte streams within one connection
Firewall issue: some networks block UDP 443 — QUIC falls back to TCP
QUIC is implemented in userspace — faster iteration than kernel TCP
